coredata.dc:title: Threaded 3D microfluidic paper analytical device-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for background-free and visual detection of organophosphorus pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Tong, Xia
Cai, Guihan
Xie, Lianwu
Wang, Tongtao
Zhu, Yongfeng
Peng, Yuqing
Tong, Chaoying
Shi, Shuyun
Guo, Ying
coredata.dc:description: With the increasing concerns of food safety and environmental protection, it is desirable to develop reliable, effective, and portable sensors for detection of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs). Here, a cascade reaction system integrated with threaded 3D microfluidic paper analytical device (3D μPAD) was firstly developed for background-free and visual detection of OPs in agricultural samples. Butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) hydrolyzed acetylcholine into thiocholine (TCh), which reduced MnO2 nanosheets into Mn2+. With addition of OPs, BChE activity was irreversibly inhibited, and the generation of TCh and the reduction of MnO2 nanosheets were prevented. Then the remaining MnO2 nanosheets oxidized o-phenylenediamine into 2,3-diaminophenazine with yellow-emission fluorescence, which quenched the fluorescence intensity of red-emission carbon dots (RCDs) via inner-filter effect. Based on above mechanism, a ratiometric fluorescent system was established for OPs detection. Threaded 3D μPAD consisted of 4 layers, which allowed to load and/or add reagents to trigger the cascade reaction system for OPs detection. The fluorescent images presented distinguishable color variations from red to yellow with dichlorvos concentrations ranging from 2.5 to 120 μg L−1, and the limit of detection was 1.0 μg L−1. In the practical samples testing, threaded 3D μPAD can eliminate background influence on fluorescent signal for OPs detection. Threaded 3D μPAD integrated with ratiometric sensing platform has merits of accuracy response, facile operation, and background-free detection, which supplies a new alternative approach for on-site pesticide detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Threaded 3D microfluidic paper device
Ratiometric fluorescent system
Organophosphorus pesticide
Background-free analysis
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of RCDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Design and fabrication of threaded 3D μPAD
Assay procedures of threaded 3D μPAD for DDVP
Characterization of RCDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Optimizing reaction conditions of threaded 3D μPAD for OPs detection
The performance of threaded 3D μPAD for OPs
Selectivity of threaded 3D μPAD for OPs
Threaded 3D μPAD for DDVP detection in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the fabrication of 2D μPAD. (B) Fluorescent images of 2D μPAD (left) and filter paper in 2D μPAD (right) with addition of RCDs, oxOPD, and rhodamine B. (C) Fabrication of threaded 3D μPAD.

(A) TEM image (inset: HR-TEM image), (B) XPS survey spectrum, and (C) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra (insets: RCDs images under daylight and 365 nm illumination) of RCDs. (D) TEM image (inset: HR-TEM image) of MnO2 nanosheets, (E) HR-XPS spectrum of Mn2p, and (F) UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets (inset: the photograph of MnO2 nanosheets under daylight).

(A) Diagram illustration of ratiometric fluorescent sensor for OPs detection. (B) Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of RCDs, UV–vis absorption spectrum of oxOPD. (C) Fluorescent lifetime curves of RCDs in the absence and presence of oxOPD.
The calibration curve of G/R ratio versus logarithm of DDVP concentration (2.5–120 μg L−1) (insets: the photographs of threaded 3D μPAD for DDVP detection).

(A) G/R ratio and photographs of threaded 3D μPAD with addition of various substances. (B) G/R ratio and photographs of threaded 3D μPAD detection system in the presence of interfering substances and DDVP.

UV–vis (A) and fluorescent spectra (B) of spinach extract solution with different times dilutions. (C) The images of spinach extract solution and extract solution on 2D μPADs under daylight (upper) and UV lamp (365 nm excitation, bottom) with different times dilutions. (D) The images of the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd layers of threaded 3D μPADs with addition of negative control, Tris-HCl buffer (6 μL, upper), and spinach extract solution (6 μL, bottom) under daylight and UV lamp (365 nm excitation). (E) The fluorescent images of threaded 3D μPADs after addition of sample extract (6 μL) and Tris-HCl buffer (6 μL), and 2D μPADs after addition of sample extract (6 μL) by spiking with DDVP (0 μg L−1 (left) and 120 μg L−1 (right)).

head.ce:title: Threaded 3D microfluidic paper analytical device-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor for background-free and visual detection of organophosphorus pesticides

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Sciences, Central South University of Forestry and Technology, Changsha, 410004, Hunan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central South University of Forestry and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increasing concerns of food safety and environmental protection, it is desirable to develop reliable, effective, and portable sensors for detection of organophosphorus pesticides (OPs). Here, a cascade reaction system integrated with threaded 3D microfluidic paper analytical device (3D μPAD) was firstly developed for background-free and visual detection of OPs in agricultural samples. Butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) hydrolyzed acetylcholine into thiocholine (TCh), which reduced MnO2 nanosheets into Mn2+. With addition of OPs, BChE activity was irreversibly inhibited, and the generation of TCh and the reduction of MnO2 nanosheets were prevented. Then the remaining MnO2 nanosheets oxidized o-phenylenediamine into 2,3-diaminophenazine with yellow-emission fluorescence, which quenched the fluorescence intensity of red-emission carbon dots (RCDs) via inner-filter effect. Based on above mechanism, a ratiometric fluorescent system was established for OPs detection. Threaded 3D μPAD consisted of 4 layers, which allowed to load and/or add reagents to trigger the cascade reaction system for OPs detection. The fluorescent images presented distinguishable color variations from red to yellow with dichlorvos concentrations ranging from 2.5 to 120 μg L−1, and the limit of detection was 1.0 μg L−1. In the practical samples testing, threaded 3D μPAD can eliminate background influence on fluorescent signal for OPs detection. Threaded 3D μPAD integrated with ratiometric sensing platform has merits of accuracy response, facile operation, and background-free detection, which supplies a new alternative approach for on-site pesticide detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides, especially organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), are widely used to kill insects and improve agricultural production (Yang et al., 2021). The pesticide residuals in crop products, water system and soils enter and accumulate in body through food chain, and pose serious threat to human health and ecosystem (Qi et al., 2021). Quick, Easy, Cheap, Effective, Rugged, and Safe (QuEChERS) as an extensive pretreatment method always involves three steps: extraction by organic solvents (e.g., acetonitrile), partitioning with salt mixture, and clear-up with adsorber (Nasiri et al., 2020; Samsidar et al., 2018). In extraction step, pigments with bright colorimetric and fluorescent signals such as chlorophyl, carotene were extracted simultaneously, and they interfere pesticide detection in colorimetry or fluorometric methods (Li et al., 2021a). The multistep sample pretreatment procedures usually result in lower enrichment factors and higher experimental errors (Nasiri et al., 2020; Samsidar et al., 2018). Thus, development of facile operation, high efficiency, and background-free procedures for sample preparation and detection are still a big challenge.

Various techniques have been explored for pesticide detection via laboratory-based equipment including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography and mass spectrometry (MS) (Cao et al., 2020). They are precise and effective, but time-consuming treatment and sophisticated instruments make them not suitable for on-site rapid detection (Montali et al., 2020; Qi et al., 2018). Currently, fluorescent, colorimetric, chemiluminescent, and electrochemistry assays have been developed for convenient and reliable pesticide analysis (Arduini et al., 2019; Montali et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2018). Among them, carbon dots (CDs)-based fluorescent system have attracted more attentions to monitor pesticides with outstanding photostability, tunable emission, high sensitivity, and low detection limit (Yan et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2022a). Comparing to single-emission system for pesticide detection, ratiometric detection system can reduce false positives, overcome environmental interference, and broaden color range (Tong et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2022b). Furthermore, ratiometric system combination with facile platforms (e.g., microfluidic paper analytical devices (μPADs), smartphone reading) would be beneficial for on-site and visual pesticide detection (Han et al., 2022; Su et al., 2021a; Zhang et al., 2020).

μPADs, as promising miniature laboratory analysis systems, have merits of easy fabrication, user-friendly operation, and small amount of volume (Lin et al., 2022; Modha et al., 2021; Noviana et al., 2021). 2D μPADs are facile to operate, while they are limited to deal with complex reaction procedures (Alahmad et al., 2021; Kim et al., 2021; Jin et al., 2020a). 3D μPADs not only transport fluids in horizontal direction, but also introduce vertical direction to separate and reconnect various substances between different layers (Lee et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2022). 3D μPADs are beneficial for multistep assays and simultaneous detection, and can carry out complex process including filtration, extraction, and mixing (Boonkaew et al., 2021). They have been applied for point-of-care diagnostics, food safety, environmental monitoring and wearable technology (Li et al., 2021b; Manmana et al., 2021; Qin et al., 2021). 3D μPADs have been compatible with electrochemistry and chemiluminescent biosensors for pesticide detection, and the combination of 3D μPADs and fluorometry sensors has not been reported for pesticide analysis (Arduini et al., 2019; Montali et al., 2020).

In this work, a ratiometric fluorescent strategy integrating with threaded 3D μPAD was firstly developed for OPs detection in agricultural samples. Red-emissive CDs (RCDs, Em. maximum 680 nm) were prepared by solvothermal treatment of cilantro. Dichlorvos (DDVP), a common OP, specifically inhibited butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) activity to trigger cascade reactions in detection system. With the increment of DDVP concentrations, the fluorescent images of threaded 3D μPAD visually ranged from red to yellow. In real sample analysis, threaded 3D μPAD effectively avoided the interference of sample pigments for OPs detection. The excellent performance of threaded 3D μPAD provided an accurate, instrument-free, and background-free platform for OPs detection and exhibited great potential in food safety, medical diagnosis and environmental monitoring fields.
Details of reagents and apparatus are described in the Electronic Supplementary Materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of RCDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Dried cilantro (0.1 g) and ethanol (10 mL) were mixed and refluxed at 90 °C for 15 h under N2 protection. After that, filtrate was evaporated to dry, dispersed in water (20 mL), and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min. The precipitate was dispersed in H2N-PEG-NH2 solution (0.6 mg mL−1) and reacted at 75 °C for 4 h, which was then purified by dialysis (500 Da) against ultrapure water for 3 days. Finally, RCDs were freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C.

MnO2 nanosheets were prepared according to previous report with minor modifications (Jin et al., 2020b). Typically, BSA (2.0 mg) was mixed with MnCl2⋅4H2O (4 mg, 10 mL) and stirred for 1 h at 25 °C, and then adjusted by NaOH (1.0 M) to pH 10. After reacting at 4 °C for 7 h, the solution was centrifuged, and washed with ultrapure water for three times. The as-prepared MnO2 nanosheets were stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of threaded 3D μPAD
ce:section.ce:para: Schematics for fabrication of threaded 3D μPAD are displayed in Fig. 1 . PVC labels (10 mm × 10 mm, thickness 104 μm) were punched with a 6.0 mm × 6.0 mm circular shape on the center by handcrafted punchers, and two PVC labels were adhered to both sides of Whatman 5 filter paper (diameter 8.0 mm) to prepare 2D μPAD (Fig. 1A). As shown in Figs. 1B and 2 D μPADs exhibit good uniformity, and the working area of filter paper was fully filled after addition of 6 μL of RCDs, 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD), or rhodamine B solution. The adhesive PVC labels are hydrophobic barriers, which prevents the solvent from spreading outside of the working area of filter paper. Here, to obtain robust fluorescent images with smartphone, a self-made portable device was designed by a UV lamp, a dark box, and a smartphone (Fig. S1).

On the top side of 2D μPADs, a circular shape (diameter 1.0 mm) was punched, and four 2D μPADs (i.e., 1st−4th layer) were strung together via cotton thread to prepare threaded 3D μPAD (Fig. 1A and C). The 1st layer was left for loading sample and BChE. For the 2nd layer, acetylcholine (ATCh, 15 mM, 6 μL) in Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0) was added. For the 3rd layer, MnO2 nanosheets (6 μL, 0.3 mg mL−1) in Tris-HCl buffer were introduced followed by RCDs (0.2 mg mL−1, 6 μL). The 4th layer was loaded with o-phenylenediamine (OPD, 0.1 M, 6 μL). Then each layer was dried at 25 °C for 15 min and kept at −20 °C in dark place for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay procedures of threaded 3D μPAD for DDVP
ce:section.ce:para: Detailed DDVP assay procedures by threaded 3D μPAD were as follows. DDVP (6 μL) with various concentrations was added on the 1st layer and set for 10 min at 25 °C for organic solvent evaporation. Then, BChE (400 U L−1, 6 μL) was added on the back of the 1st layer and incubated for 15 min at 37 °C. The 1st layer was then covered on the 2nd layer, and H2O (12 μL) was added to elute BChE from the 1st layer to the 2nd layer to trigger the reaction between BChE and ATCh at 37 °C for 15 min. After that, the 2nd layer was stacked on the 3rd layer, and H2O (12 μL) was dropped to transfer hydrolysis product thiocholine (TCh) to the 3rd layer. On 3rd layer, TCh reduced MnO2 nanosheets at 37 °C for 10 min. After reaction, the 3rd layer was located on the 4th layer, and H2O (12 μL) was added on the back of the 4th layer to transfer OPD from the 4th layer to the 3rd layer to reduce remaining MnO2 nanosheets at 37 °C for 10 min. Finally, the 3rd layer was placed on portable device to obtain fluorescent images via smartphone camera. The RGB values were analyzed by “Color Recognizer” application in smartphone.

Spinach or tomato (5.0 g) was smashed and ultrasonically extracted with acetonitrile (10 mL) for 30 min, which was then filtered (0.22 μm) to gain extract solution. Extract solution (6 μL) was added onto the 1st layer of threaded 3D μPAD, and then the following steps were the same with the described procedures for DDVP assay. For recovery tests, DDVP with different concentrations (5 μg L−1, 25 μg L−1, 80 μg L−1) was sprayed and evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of RCDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Environmentally friendly and easy-obtained natural biomasses have been considered as outstanding starting materials for CDs synthesis (Tong et al., 2022). During hydrothermal treatment of natural biomasses, active compounds were extracted, polymerized, and carbonized to form CDs. Optimum optical properties for RCDs were achieved when refluxing dried cilantro (0.1 g) with ethanol (10 mL) at 90 °C for 15 h (Fig. S2). The morphology and size of RCDs were studied via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). RCDs are spherical and well-dispersed with average diameter of 5.5 nm (Fig. 2A and S3). In high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image, the lattice spacing of RCDs was 0.21 nm, assigning to (100) facet of graphitic carbon (Bogireddy et al., 2021; Rao et al., 2020). XRD pattern (Fig. S4) has a broad peak at 21.6° due to sp2 hybridization of amorphous carbon in RCDs (Tong et al., 2022). Raman spectrum of RCDs (Fig. S5) exhibits two peaks at 1359 and 1535 cm−1, corresponding to D band (disordered sp3 carbon) and G band (graphitic sp2 carbon). The ID/IG ratio is 1.12, indicating the presence of amorphous structures in RCDs (Hu and Gao, 2020). The surface functional groups and element analysis of RCDs were measured through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In FT-IR spectrum of RCDs, the vibrations for N–H/O–H (3400 cm−1), C–H (2925, 2841 cm−1), CO (1739 cm−1), CC/CN (1639 cm−1), C–N (1463, 1341 cm−1) and C–O–C (1110, 1042 cm−1) reveal the existence of –OH, –COOH, –NH2 groups in RCDs (Fig. S6) (Rao et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2021). The full XPS spectrum of RCDs shows three peaks at 532.1, 399.1 and 284.1 eV (Fig. 2B), indicating the presence of O (18.54%), N (4.56%) and C (76.90%) elements (Rao et al., 2020). The HR-XPS spectrum of O1s exhibits two peaks at 531.9 and 531.3 eV, which are assigned to C–O and CO (Fig. S7A) (Rao et al., 2020). The N1s deconvolution has peaks at 399.4 eV for graphitic-N/amine-N and 398.7 eV for pyridinic-N (Fig. S7B) (Bogireddy et al., 2021). For C1s spectrum, four peaks are assigned to CO (287.9 eV), C–N (285.1 eV), C–O/CN (284.5 eV) and C–C/CC (283.9 eV) (Fig. S7C) (Bogireddy et al., 2021). The FT-IR and XPS results of RCDs prove that RCDs were functionalized with abundant O and N-containing groups (Jia et al., 2021). The UV–vis spectrum of RCDs exhibits absorption bands at 280 (π-π* transition of conjugated CC), 324 and 420 (n-π* transition of conjugated CO/CN), and 673 nm (n-π* transition of chlorophyll-derived porphyrin) (Fig. 2C) (Tong et al., 2021). The maximum emission intensity of RCDs was located at 680 nm under 365 nm excitation, and RCDs solution shows bright red fluorescent emission (Fig. 2C, inset). RCDs display excitation-independent property with excitation wavelengths from 325–385 nm (Fig. S8). The quantum yield (QY) of RCDs was calculated as 6.1% by using Rhodamine B as reference (Tong et al., 2021).

MnO2 nanosheets display a typical 2D sheet-like morphology with numerous wrinkles and folds (Fig. 2D). In HR-TEM image, the lattice fringe with interplanar spacing of 0.23 nm is corresponding to (420) plane of MnO2 (Fig. 2D, inset) (Zhang et al., 2021). The diffraction peaks at 18.3°, 28.9°, 36.2° and 65.0° in XRD pattern are ascribed to (002), (003), (100) and (110) planes (Fig. S9) (Zhang et al., 2021). The Mn–O stretching vibration was observed at 500 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S10) (Wang et al., 2021). The chemical composition of MnO2 nanosheets was characterized via XPS spectrum (Fig. S11), and HR-XPS spectrum of Mn2p exhibits two characteristic peaks at 652.8 and 641.1 eV, assigning to Mn2p1/2 and Mn2p3/2 (Fig. 2E) (Han et al., 2018). UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets exhibits a broad band ranging from 250 nm to 700 nm (Fig. 2F).

MnO2 nanosheets, as a common oxidase-like and peroxidase-like nanozyme, have been applied to participate many catalytic oxidation reactions (Han et al., 2018; Jin et al., 2020b; Wu et al., 2021). MnO2 nanosheets oxidize non-fluorescent OPD into yellow fluorescent oxOPD (Wu et al., 2021). Therefore, in the MnO2 nanosheets + OPD system, UV–vis absorption peak for oxOPD occurs (Fig. S12). Based on MnO2 nanosheets and BChE, a cascade reaction system was constructed, and the principle for OPs determination is manifested in Fig. 3 A. In the presence of BChE, ATCh is catalyzed into TCh, which triggers the destruction of MnO2 nanosheets into Mn2+ and blocks the oxidation of OPD. The decreasing UV–vis absorption of MnO2 nanosheets and the absent absorption for oxOPD in BChE + ATCh + MnO2+OPD system indicate that MnO2 nanosheets have been completely reduced by TCh (Fig. S12). In the presence of OPs, BChE activity is inhibited, and less MnO2 nanosheets are decomposed. The remaining MnO2 nanosheets oxidate OPD into oxOPD, and fluorescent intensity of RCDs is weakened (Fig. S13).

The fluorescent quenching mechanism of RCDs by oxOPD was investigated. In Fig. 3B, the large spectral overlap between oxOPD (Abs. maximum 420 nm) and RCDs (Ex. maximum 365 nm) indicates that the sensing mechanism may be inner-filter effect (IFE) rather than Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) owing to no overlap between oxOPD absorption and RCDs emission spectrum. The fluorescent lifetime of RCDs was measured in the absence and presence of oxOPD, which is 5.84 ns and 5.73 ns (Fig. 3C). The almost invariable lifetimes rule out FRET and photo-induced electron transfer process. No new absorption peak appears in UV–vis spectrum of the mixture of RCDs and oxOPD (Fig. S14), which excludes the static quenching effect. Thus, the fluorescent quenching mechanism of RCDs by oxOPD is assigned to IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing reaction conditions of threaded 3D μPAD for OPs detection
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the accuracy, efficiency, and stability of threaded 3D μPAD for measuring OPs, reaction parameters were optimized. Quantitative filter paper (medium and fast flow), and Whatman 4 and 5 filter papers were applied for the preparation of 2D μPADs, and rhodamine B aqueous solution was selected to test the property. Obviously, using Whatman 5 filter paper, uniform distribution and impregnation images are obtained in single and layer-to-layer paper (Figs. S15A and B). Next, the volume of 6 μL for single layer, and 12 μL for layer-to-layer μPADs can fill with working area (Figs. S15C and D). Then, the concentration of substances on different layers was optimized (Fig. S16). The suitable concentrations of RCDs, MnO2 nanosheets, and OPD were found to be 0.20 mg mL−1, 0.3 mg mL−1, and 100 mM. Furthermore, the reaction conditions of BChE were tested (Fig. S16). The optimal conditions for BChE were 15 mM of ATCh reaction at 37 °C for 15 min in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.0, 10 mM). G/R ratio exhibits linear relationship with BChE activity ranging from 50–400 U L−1 (Fig. S17), and then BChE activity was set at 400 U L−1. Finally, according to distinguishable variations of fluorescent signals in yellow and red regions (Figs. S18 and S19), a ratiometric fluorescent system can be designed to monitor OPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of threaded 3D μPAD for OPs
ce:section.ce:para:
DDVP, a common and efficient OP, was used as a model to evaluate the analytical performance of threaded 3D μPAD. The fluorescent color of the 3rd layer of threaded 3D μPAD displays noticeable changes from red to yellow, and G/R ratio is increasing with DDVP concentrations from 2.5 to 120 μg L−1 (Fig. 4 ). A good linear relationship was acquired between G/R ratio and logarithm of DDVP concentration (R
2 0.9899) in the range of 2.5–120 μg L−1. The calibration curve was y = 0.2265x + 0.7007, and LOD was calculated to be 1.0 μg L−1 based on signal-to-noise ratio 3. The maximal residue limit (MRL) of DDVP in food is stipulated from 0.05 to 0.5 mg kg−1 by the National Health Commission of the People's Republic of China (GB 2763-2021). If 1 g of food sample is extracted with 1 mL of solution, MRL is calculated to be 50–500 μg L−1. Thus, this method meets the determination requirements, and can be applied for point-of-care testing in daily life. Threaded 3D μPADs were stored for 30 days to evaluate the stability for DDVP detection (Table S1). Their linear ranges and LODs keep seldom change, and accurate detection results with high precision (inter-assay coefficient of variation (CV), less than 8.3%) present. Moreover, repeatability shows among personnel operations (Table S2). Results indicate that threaded 3D μPAD have good durability, excellent repeatability, satisfactory stability, and high sensitivity for DDVP detection. Comparing with reported methods (Table S3), developed ratiometric fluorescent system integrated with threaded 3D μPAD presents comparable sensitivity, broad color variation range, on-site and background-free detection without complex sample pretreatment process, which is appropriate in daily point-of-use applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of threaded 3D μPAD for OPs
ce:section.ce:para: Some common substances, including metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+), small molecules (Glucose, Glu; L-Phenylalanine, L-Phe; L-Leucine, L-Leu), macromolecules (bovine serum albumin, BSA; alkaline phosphatase, ALP), non-OPs (2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid, 2,4-D; fipronil) and OPs (DDVP, parathion-methyl; dimethoate; parathion; glyphosate) were selected to investigate the selectivity of threaded 3D μPAD for OPs. In Fig. 5 A, the remarkable color changes were observed toward OPs, while other potential interferences displayed no significant response. The obvious differences between OPs and non-OPs suggest the high selectivity. As displayed in Fig. 5B, the coexistence of interference substances and DDVP has negligible influence on color responses and G/R ratio of detection system, indicating strong anti-interference ability for DDVP. In Fig. S20, RCDs show good photostability in pH (7.0–9.0) and NaCl (0–1.0 M), and even irradiation under UV lamp (365 nm) for 150 min. Thus, good selectivity, strong interference ability, and excellent photostability suggest the great potential for OPs detection in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Threaded 3D μPAD for DDVP detection in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicability of threaded 3D μPAD for DDVP detection, spinach and tomato were chosen as real samples. Acetonitrile was applied to extract DDVP in spinach. Spinach extract solution contains the characteristic UV–vis absorption and fluorescent emission for pigments (e.g., chlorophyll) (Fig. 6 A and B), which results in significant background interferences for OPs assay. Extract solution exhibits obvious green color (under daylight) and bright red fluorescence (under UV lamp, 365 nm) on filter paper (Fig. 6C), and the phenomena are still observed even after 100-fold dilution. With the addition of extract solution on the 1st layer of threaded 3D μPAD, green color and red fluorescence can be obviously observed on the 1st and 2nd layer and be negligible on the 3rd layer (Fig. 6D). In Fig. 6E, the results of threaded 3D μPAD for DDVP detection in sample solution (G/R: 0.70 for 0 μg L−1; 1.17 for 120 μg L−1) are consistent with the results in Tris-HCl buffer (G/R: 0.71 for 0 μg L−1; 1.18 for 120 μg L−1). However, serious background interferences were obviously observed by using 2D μPADs for DDVP detection (G/R: 0.54 for 0 μg L−1; 0.58 for 120 μg L−1). Thus, threaded 3D μPAD can eliminate background interferences effectively. In addition, the evaporation of extract solution, acetonitrile, on the 1st layer avoids the organic solvent influence on BChE activity (Jin et al., 2020a). Up to date, aptamer electrochemical system and upconversion nanoparticles/polydopamine-based hydrogel suit have been proposed to eliminating background interference (Qi et al., 2021; Su et al., 2021b). Here, threaded 3D μPAD can be directly employed for visual and background-free detection of OPs without complex sample pretreatment process, expensive instruments, and delicate materials.

It was obvious in Table S4, no DDVP residue was detected in spinach and tomato samples. The recoveries ranged from 94.0% to 106.0% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) lower than 8.6%. Detection results keep consistent with those by HPLC−MS. The excellent and satisfactory performance of the proposed method indicates that it is suitable for reliable and on-site OPs detection in real samples.

In summary, a threaded 3D μPAD was firstly constructed based on cascade reaction system for sensitive, background-free, and on-site ratiometric fluorescent detection of OPs in agricultural samples. The reaction reagents were loaded on the 1st−4th layers of threaded 3D μPAD, and the distinct color transition from red to yellow was observed with the increment of OPs concentration. What is worth mentioning is that the multi-layers structure of threaded 3D μPAD effectively avoids background interference from real samples. This study broadens the applications of fluorescent assay for pesticide residuals, and the design diversity of 3D μPAD supplies powerful tool for cost-effective platform for sample-in-result-out pesticide detection in complex agricultural samples. However, many OPs exhibit inhibition efficiency to BChE, and array-based biosensors can be designed to generate multidimensional data to differentiate OPs according to their sensitivity to BChE.

Xia Tong: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Guihan Cai: Formal analysis, Investigation. Lianwu Xie: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Tongtao Wang: Validation, Visualization. Yongfeng Zhu: Data curation, Investigation. Yuqing Peng: Validation, Data curation. Chaoying Tong: Investigation. Shuyun Shi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Ying Guo: Methodology, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Key Research and Development projects in Hunan Province, China (2021NK2024 and 2020NK2019), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South University, China (2021zzts0067).
